Japanese "Standing" Sushi
description: shorts #japan #sushi.
full_description: #shorts #japan #sushi

transcript_segments.text:
Let's go!! This is called standing sushi, and it's a type of meal in Japan
where you stand at a bar and eat whatever the chef serves you. Our chef started the night by chugging
a beer and a whole glass of wine, which I'd definitely never seen before. The meal began with a huge block of tuna, which the chef cut down
into smaller pieces, shaped into nigiri,
grated on some fresh black truffle, and then topped with some buttery,
melted bone marrow. Next,
he brought out some beautiful mackerel, which he torched until it became
charred and crispy around the edges. Fire!! He sliced it up and served
it with some crispy seaweed. For the palate cleanser,
he brought out some sea urchin, also known as uni, and made a massive sushi roll
that probably had about $500 worth of sea urchin inside. I got really excited when he
brought out my favorite sushi fish, a black-throat sea perch... Just keep swimming. Just keep swimming. which he quickly filleted, then carefully skewered and grilled
over some special Japanese charcoal. For dessert,
we had this special Japanese orange, which he peeled in the
coolest way possible. And it tasted even better than it looked. Bye-bye!
I love you!

full_description:
Welcome to ‪‪ @tamo__tyan ‬ ‬Channel!

On this channel, we showcase some of Japan’s best and most delicious sweets and foods!

【Cute Fish-Shaped Wagashi 🐟】

Wakaayu (Young Sweetfish) - 200 yen
This traditional Japanese sweet, shaped like a small fish, has been crafted by skilled artisans for generations. It’s a seasonal delicacy that beautifully captures the essence of summer in Japan. In some regions, it’s cherished as a summer festival favorite and makes a lovely gift or souvenir.

Our Wakaayu features a subtle hint of soy sauce in the dough, adding a savory aroma that pairs perfectly with its chewy, mochi-like texture. The combination of flavors and textures makes it truly irresistible!

You'll find a variety of other wagashi sweets at the shop—perfect for those with a sweet tooth! 😋

Store: Kanoya Confectionery
Address: 24 Tanaeda, Wakamatsu Higashimachi, Toyota City, Aichi Prefecture
Access: 5-minute walk from Wakarimachi Station
Hours: 8:00 AM – 6:00 PM (closes at 5:00 PM on Sundays)
Closed: Mondays
Price Range: ¥1,000 – ¥2,000

title: I tried eating 10 pounds of noodles in Japan...

transcript_segments.text: If you know anything about food challenges in Japan, you'd know that they don't mess around here. A little while back, I attempted an 11P lb ramen challenge and got uh low-key smoked. However, I found this spot in Tokyo called Buddha Buddha that offers a 10 lb soupless ramen challenge. So, it's time for round two, son. Now, they say it's soupless, but in addition to the nine servings of noodles, they also fill up about a third of the bowl with té and oil. As for the toppings, each bowl comes with green onions, corn, menma, chashu, some guoza, a fishcake, seaweed, a pile of spicy miso, and some sesame seeds. Oh yeah. After getting started, you'll also discover that they hide an entire chashu log in that boy as well. In order to win, you have to eat everything in the bowl within 25 minutes. So, I got straight down to business. This was my first time trying a Buddha, and the flavor of the mixed noodles was super potent. Apparently, there was some dude named Ryu who had smoked the entire bowl in under 14 minutes. So, I kept on pushing, but I quickly found out that the whole idea of if you eat everything fast enough, your brain won't realize you're full is a scam. Japan 3. Josh zero.

title: The most unwelcoming restaurant in all of Japan

transcript_segments.text: this might be the most unfriendly restaurant in all of Japan and it's getting absolutely demolished by online reviews apparently everybody's upset with the cleanliness of the restaurant and the attitude of the chef but absolutely nobody's complaining about the food this is Tako and I don't blame you for not knowing that because there's absolutely zero signage indicating that this is a restaurant just a branch that has we open carved into it even the building is unwelcoming when I walked in I wasn't acknowledged welcomed or even told where to sit I don't normally care but being straight up ignored is a pretty dismal feeling but then I watched the same thing happen to each person that walked in regardless of if they were in Katamari cosplay like I was or not I told my order to the chef and was completely ignored again no reply no confirmation no nothing literal silence but then I watched again as it happened to everyone and sure enough he cooked up my food with a blazing speed this restaurant isn't for the squeamish it's not for those who are worried about hygiene risks it's not for those who need to be waited on and it certainly isn't for people who like talking but it is for people who want aggressively flavored Fried Chicken it's coated in Pepper before it's fried which makes it spicy but in a weird way and the fried rice has stopped Notch when I left he hit me with an extremely warm and genuine thank you and that made me happy

transcript_segments.text: I'm at ichir on Ramen the one restaurant you don't have to make eye contact or talk with anyone let's take a look inside after ordering on the machine you can have a seat and begin customizing your Ramen the staff will then serve you through this bamboo curtain and close it so that you can eat in private the ramen is served with two slices of chashu pork you can request as much of their spicy red sauce as you'd like and trust me when I say it has a nice kick if you remove the barrier you can talk with a friend or maybe make a new one pro tip you can order a second serving of noodles simply pay at the machine and place your card on top of the button I was pleasantly surprised that the noodles were seasoned from the time you walk in until the time you leave they have English support everywhere now if you'll excuse me I need to get back to my Ramen

title: Japan Restaurant That Costs Less to Eat MORE… 🤯

full_description: #japan #shorts #food #interestingfacts This is the restaurant called Hachikyo, where it costs more when you eat less.

transcript_segments.text: There's a restaurant that punishes you just for leaving food on your plate. It's called Aikio, a famous Japanese seafood spot known for its massive bowls of salmon row. And before you even order, they make you agree to a crazy rule. If you don't finish every grain of rice, you have to pay a fine. And it might sound extreme, but it's not about money. It's about respect for the hundreds of fishermen risking their lives at sea. At Aikia, wasting food isn't just careless, it's disrespectful.

title: How living in Japan changed me ☔️
This ramen shop in Japan has NO MENU

transcript_segments.text: This ramen restaurant doesn't have a menu, and you don't even have to order. You just sit down, get what you're served, eat, and leave. And what you get is a pretty phenomenal bowl, and it makes me wonder why more restaurants don't operate like this. It's just easier for everybody. I'm walking around Osaka at a place called Ju Higashi, and it's kind of known as a rougher area. Well, rough by Japanese standards. The ball pit at McDonald's is rough by Japanese standards. Either way, this is ton, and they don't serve ton men, so don't even think about ordering it. In fact, don't even order at all. Several people around me tried, but they ended up looking like noobs. All you have to do is take a seat and not say a word. I run into places all the time with only one thing on the menu, but this place doesn't even have a menu. It just has a sign that says the price is 900 yen. They don't serve drinks, no fried rice, no gyoza, not even the ability to order a large or a smaller size. And apparently, it's been that way for over 40 years. Everyone gets the exact same thing. And if you don't like it, don't come here. And what you get is a masterfully prepared bowl of Tonko ramen. It wasn't too stinky with that pork bone funk, nor was it overly garlicky or anything like that. But you can get free toppings like kimchi. So, this is a winner in my book. This was a pretty unique experience.

It was the best $7 I ever spent.

I'm here in Japan to buy a house. But to be happy, I'm going to need friends. So I rented an old man in Tokyo, who taught me about Japanese culture, food, dating, work culture and real estate in Tokyo. The site is called Ossan Rental, and I highly recommend renting an old man the next time you're in Japan.

transcript_segments.text: I'm about to rent an old man Japan being Japan it wasn't entirely surprising to find that old people are available for rent the website for this is called Osan rental Osan means old man you can choose a normal old man a kind old man a cultural old man or even a small snugly old man I wasn't sure what kind of old man I wanted but I knew I didn't want this snugly one so I added a few of them to my cart I wasn't expecting response as I personally would not respond to an email from a stranger asking to rent me but one of them did respond sakur I was a little thrown off when I met Sakurai because despite being 58 years old he looked much younger he' made a good living and retired early then spent his free time playing tennis and cooking elaborate meals for his family but he found himself looking for something more so he put himself on the market now people hire him to play sports go to movies and Shop with them sometimes they take him drinking or on long drives but never at the same time he's not available for illegal or evasive actions I asked him how I can make friends in Japan he suggested I learned the language but what I discovered today is that there's another easier way to make friends renting an old man

title: Japanese Street Food in Osaka!! 🇯🇵 KING OF TUNA + 5 Foods You Have to Try in Osaka, Japan!
description: Best Restaurants in Osaka: https://youtu.be/2s-m2XeTcH4 SUBSCRIBE for more: https://www.youtube.com/@MarkWiens ...
full_description:
🇯🇵 Best Restaurants in Osaka: https://youtu.be/2s-m2XeTcH4
👉 SUBSCRIBE for more: https://www.youtube.com/@MarkWiens 
👕 T-shirts: https://store.migrationology.com/  

OSAKA, JAPAN - Welcome to Osaka, one of the food capitals of Japan and known for their amazing Japanese street food. Today I’ll be taking you to eat at 5 places that serve legendary Japanese street food! 

Nonkiya (のんきや https://maps.app.goo.gl/CaZtvFyirdokWYBJ7) - We’re beginning this tour today with a standing bar that specializes in both oden as well as skewers of tendon simmered in miso sauce. It was delicious and a great heritage restaurant. Price - 1,420 JPY ($8.83)

Matsuya (立ち食いうどんそば 松屋 https://maps.app.goo.gl/TRW3RrB5Xe8eYEbZ7) - Right next to Nonkiya, you’ll find Matsuya, a standing noodle bar that serves signature Osaka style udon noodles with sweet bean curd. There’s no seating, so you jump in, order, and stand to eat. This is real deal Japanese fast food. Price - 250 JPY ($1.55) per bowl

Tengu (てんぐ https://maps.app.goo.gl/hABpfCBmBck7VkpLA ) - One of the quintessential Japanese foods of Osaka is called kushikatsu, an assortment of ingredients on skewered, breaded and deep fried. With some beer, things couldn’t be better. Price - 8,420 JPY ($52.35) for 5 people

Hanadako (はなだこ https://maps.app.goo.gl/3czHzjZBe3G7rtNb6) - When it comes to Osaka street food, nothing is more famous or more popular than takoyaki, octopus batter balls. And ask locals where to eat the best takoyaki in Osaka, and many will point you to Hanadako. Price - 860 JPY ($5.35) for my plate

Izakaya Toyo (居酒屋とよ https://maps.app.goo.gl/WdmsmYdk57amq8B39) - Finally, there was no where else I was more excited to visit on this Japanese food tour in Osaka than Izakaya Toyo, where Mr. Toyo is legendary for his blow torched tuna cheek that he cooks with his bare hands! Price - 8,500 JPY ($52.85) for everything 

When you’re in Osaka, you have a lot of delicious food to try, but don’t miss these 5 classic foods!

title: The Most Legendary 3-Michelin Star Restaurant in Japan
description: Hidden amidst the tranquil green setting of Kyoto, lies Kikunoi Honten, a world-renowned 3-Michelin Star kaiseki restaurant that ...
full_description:
Hidden amidst the tranquil green setting of Kyoto, lies Kikunoi Honten, a world-renowned 3-Michelin Star kaiseki restaurant that has served guests traditional Japanese cuisine for over 100 years.

Housed in a mansion that resembles a samurai residence and teahouse, Kikunoi's dining atmosphere is like something I've never seen. From traditional tatami mat rooms with unique flower arrangements and hanging scrolls, to the views of beautiful gardens that surround the estate, I was in awe the second I stepped inside.

The food served at Kikunoi is on a level I couldn't imagine. Each course is prepared with ingredients that can only be harvested 10 days of out of the year, and the menu changes constantly to reflect the ebb and flow of nature and its seasons. 35 cooks fill the kitchen, each focused on a single task. 

It was a sight to behold.

I am beyond honored to have been able to spend time in such close quarters with the team inside their establishment - every second was a dream to film.

Yoshihiro Murata is at the helm, leading his brigade of chefs and cooks to something exquisite and beautiful. I met and talked with many of the staff - they were all so kind, inviting, warm, and fiercely talented in the kitchen. 

I also want to thank the talented composers and artists whose music I used in this video. Creating scenes with their soundtrack was pure magic.
@periwinklepopo

@BigRicePiano 

After almost a year of editing, I'm proud to share this story with you all.

TOKYO, JAPAN - Today I’m in Tokyo along with my friend Abram, Ramen Beast (follow him: https://www.instagram.com/ramen_beast/). He’s eaten over 4,000 bowls of ramen across Japan and he’s taking us to 3 of his favorites in Tokyo. We’re in for some top level Japanese ramen today, and a nice variety of different styles and flavors. 

Ramen is one of the ultimate Japanese foods to eat and something I love about it is there’s so much freedom and every ramen restaurant has its own unique character, personality, and style.

Places we went: 
00:00 Intro
01:24 E-chan - Japanese Ramen 
14:07 Aidaya - Japanese Tsukemen, Dipping Ramen 
27:42 Shibuya Crossing 
28:38 Lashowhan - Extreme Spicy Chinese Ramen

E-chan (えーちゃん食堂 https://maps.app.goo.gl/D7v3aPTPJGYqc3uTA) - Our first bowl of ramen was a class, good clean flavor broth with hand-made noodles. This spot only opens for breakfast and sells out in 2 - 3 hours. Price - 1,000 JPY ($6.22) per bowl 

Aidaya (あいだや https://maps.app.goo.gl/GA9Urh7UXRMyDn4R6 ) - Next up on this ramen tour in Tokyo we’re eat tsukemen, the dipping style of ramen, and this place does it extraordinarily well. The sukiyaki wagyu was a bonus! Price - 2,300 JPY ($14.30) per set 

Lashowhan (担々麺 ラーメン 辣椒漢 https://maps.app.goo.gl/Y4v9mwjt6Sx3aTSW7) - Finally, Beast takes me to eat an extremely spicy Chinese style dry ramen. Price - 1,100 JPY ($6.84) per bowl

[나카타니도 中谷堂] 
- 위치: Japan, Nara, Hashimotocho 29 
- 영업: 화 휴무, 10:00 - 19:00
- 메뉴: 요모기모찌 1개 (200엔)

This is a famous mochi shop in Nara, known for pounding yomogi mochi at incredible speed. It’s also ranked as one of Japan’s top 100 traditional shops!

The mochi is so soft that it stretches on its own just by holding it, and the chunky sweet red bean filling pairs perfectly with the fresh yomogi aroma. Whenever I think of truly delicious mochi, this place always comes to mind.

It’s located in Nara, about an hour from Osaka. A perfect spot to enjoy some yomogi mochi while visiting Nara Park and watching the deer. Highly recommended!

[Nakatani-do 中谷堂]
- Location: 29 Hashimotocho, Nara, Japan
- Hours: 10:00–19:00 (Closed on Tuesdays)
- Menu: Yomogi Mochi (200 JPY per piece)

title: What I Hate Tourists Doing in Japan
channel: Let's ask Shogo | Katana, Budo, & Kimono
description: There are so many travelers in Kyoto now as if the covid pandemic never happened. As much as I am happy that everyone is ...
full_description:
There are so many travelers in Kyoto now as if the covid pandemic never happened. As much as I am happy that everyone is visiting my favorite city in Japan, there is something I really don’t like seeing tourists do. It is… Sitting on the ground in public spaces. The act of sitting outside on the ground or floor is considered very vulgar in Japan. There are religious and cultural reasons for this, such as the fact that in Japan people sleep and sit on the floor, and if they get dirty, they will ruin their own and others' homes. Now, do Japanese people never do this? Unfortunately, some do. But if you ever have a chance to visit Japan, take a look at those who sit in front of convenience stores or near game stations. You will notice that many people have no manners and no decency. When people sit on the ground, it ruins the scenery and is disturbing for many Japanese. It may be tough sometimes, but you should stand unless you find a bench or some other place where you are allowed to sit.

*The content of this video is based on personal studies and experience. There is no intention of denying other theories and cultural aspects.

title: Best Japanese Food in Osaka!! 🇯🇵 300 Year Old Eel Restaurant! | Osaka, Japan
description: Japanese Street Food in Osaka: https://youtu.be/3zLvByt52go?si=DOjWs1-7VCsTtN5k SUBSCRIBE for more: ...
full_description:
🇯🇵 Japanese Street Food in Osaka: https://youtu.be/3zLvByt52go?si=DOjWs1-7VCsTtN5k
👉 SUBSCRIBE for more: https://www.youtube.com/@MarkWiens 
👕 T-shirts: https://store.migrationology.com/  

OSAKA, JAPAN - Osaka is a Japanese food capital, and along with eating lots of Japanese street food during my time there, I also wanted to check out a few of the best Japanese restaurants. 

Here are the restaurants I visited: 

Maruyoshi Sushi (https://maps.app.goo.gl/6MxagNxBnC7DMa616) - Located at a morning market in Osaka, this restaurant serves the freshest fish you can imagine. There are two famous places next to each other, so I chose the less famous option. Their mixed kaisen don including an assortment of seafood over rice, was fantastic. Price - 2,750 JPY ($17.10) 

Kadoya Shokudo (https://maps.app.goo.gl/USV6e3qzjTq9yQvf7) - Next up on this Japanese food tour in Osaka I went to one of the highest rated ramen restaurant, known especially for their Tsukemen, or dipping ramen. I think I got lucky with the line! Price - 2,500 JPY ($15.54)

Honke Shibato (https://maps.app.goo.gl/3e5tJc8iKau3hXvSA) - To say this restaurant is an institution is an understatement! They’ve been open for over 300 years and still run by the 15th generation. Their unagi, eel, is among the best I’ve had in Japan. Price - 8,870 JPY ($55.15) per dish

Namba Okonomiyaki Ajinoya Honten (https://maps.app.goo.gl/evhY6vpmfgNogHP59) - One of the signature Japanese dishes of Osaka is okonomiyaki, and I specifically wanted to come here because it’s an original shop that’s been open since 1965 and 4 generations, that’s still family run. Price - 8,200 JPY ($52.84) for everything 

If you love food and Japanese food, Osaka is an eating paradise. This is just a start of the countless delicious restaurants to try in Osaka!

description: 韓国旅行に行ったら一度は挑戦したい屋台おでん！ でも「どうやって注文するの？」「マナーはある？」と不安な方も多いはず。
full_description:
韓国旅行に行ったら一度は挑戦したい屋台おでん！
でも「どうやって注文するの？」「マナーはある？」と不安な方も多いはず。
これさえ覚えれば、あなたも今日から屋台マスターです✨

✅ 屋台おでんの楽しみ方
1️⃣ まずは挨拶！「オデン モゴド デヨ？（おでん食べてもいいですか？）」と声をかけよう！
2️⃣ スープは無料！ 備え付けの紙コップで自由に飲んでOK。これが最高に美味しいんです…（おかわり自由！）
3️⃣ タレのルール： 醤油は直接つけず、備え付けのカップやスプレーを使ってね。
4️⃣ だいこんは我慢！ スープの中の大きな大根は出汁用。食べるのはNGです⚠️
5️⃣ お会計： 最後に「ケサナルケヨ（お会計お願いします）」と言って、食べた串の数を見せればOK！

💰 Tip: 屋台は現金のみの場所が多いので、1,000ウォン札をいくつか準備しておくとスムーズですよ！

寒い日に外で食べる熱々のおでんは格別！
次の韓国旅行でぜひチャレンジしてみてね✨

title: ⁠Japan Food Eskalation! 🤩 | OSAKA Cinematic Food Guide 🇯🇵
description: Konnichiwa, Freunde des guten Geschmacks! ✨ Willkommen in OSAKA – der ultimativen Food-Stadt Japans! Wer Japan ...
full_description:
Konnichiwa, Freunde des guten Geschmacks! 🇯🇵✨ Willkommen in OSAKA – der ultimativen Food-Stadt Japans! Wer Japan bereist und nicht hier isst, hat definitiv was verpasst. Osaka ist wild, laut, voller Neonlichter – und kulinarisch einfach next level. 🍜🔥

Von dampfenden Ramen-Bowls über perfekt gegrillte Takoyaki bis hin zu butterzartem Wagyu – @BerndZehner durch die Straßen gefuttert und alles probiert, was Osaka zu bieten hat. Und das ist verdammt viel! 🤯 Aber es geht nicht nur um die Klassiker – wir haben auch nach den versteckten Perlen gesucht, die selbst viele Einheimische nicht auf dem Schirm haben.

Osaka ist eine Stadt, die niemals satt wird. Hier dreht sich alles um gutes Essen, gesellige Abende und ein Streetfood-Erlebnis, das man so schnell nicht vergisst. Wo gibt’s die beste Okonomiyaki? Welcher Izakaya ist ein Muss? Und lohnt sich Kobe Beef wirklich? Die Antworten gibt’s im Video!

Wart ihr schon mal in Japan oder habt ein japanisches Lieblingsgericht? Und wer freut sich auf das große Staffel Finale in Tokyo? Schreibt’s in die Kommentare! 👇💬

👉 Jeden Monat eine neue Episode unserer Cinematic Food Guides! 📅 Abonniert den Kanal, lasst ein Like da und helft uns, die 500k zu knacken! 🎉🔥

🎥 Hier geht’s zur Seoul Food Guide: https://youtu.be/jhlPbPqN_YE

#osaka #japan #foodguide

Vielen Dank auch für die Unterstützung von „Travel Japan Together“! Ohne sie wären wir nicht in der Lage gewesen, euch soviele Geheimtipps präsentieren zu können. Wenn auch ihr Osaka und viele weitere japanische Städte kulinarisch erfahren und erleben wollte, empfehlen wir euch, eine ihrer Food Touren zu buchen: https://traveljapan-together.com/

Damit du dein authentisches Kocherlebnis zelebrieren kannst, kann ein bisschen Hilfe hierbei nie schaden! Jeder Foodie braucht das richtige Zubehör.

title: Why Kamakura is a Perfect Day Trip from Tokyo, Japan!! 🇯🇵
description: Kobe Beef Bento in Japan: https://youtu.be/I7OBAusmbuo SUBSCRIBE for more: ...
full_description:
🇯🇵 Kobe Beef Bento in Japan: https://youtu.be/I7OBAusmbuo
👉 SUBSCRIBE for more: https://www.youtube.com/@MarkWiensAbroad 
👕 T-shirts: https://store.migrationology.com/ 

Today we’re taking a day trip to Kamakura from Tokyo, Japan! In this video I’ll be giving you all the details about how to take this amazing day trip and everything you should do, see, and especially eat when you visit this amazing city. Kamakura is about an hour away from Tokyo, along the coast, and it’s a perfect place for a day trip when you have an extra day in Tokyo, Japan. 

From Tokyo Station, we caught the normal train to Kita-Kamakura Station (actually the station before the main Kamakura station - we got off early in order to hike.)

Shintomi Ramen and Sushi (https://maps.app.goo.gl/L3GVo7SpDdjF9J15A) - First we stopped for some food at the nearest ramen restaurant. It was simple and good. 

Verve Coffee Roasters (https://maps.app.goo.gl/yjxoxjPkU92B3FzF9) - And before hiking I needed some energy! 

Daibutsu Hiking Trail - This was a highlight of the day, even though it was a bit muddy. It takes about an hour and there are great views and lots of nature along the way. 

Shirasu Cafe Kamakura Jinbei (https://maps.app.goo.gl/3vfKiBfRA7Ummart9) - One of the reasons I really wanted to visit Kamakura on a day trip from Tokyo was to eat shirasu - the delicacy of Kamakura which are little translucent fish called whitebait.

title: Das große FINALE in TOKIO 🇯🇵 | Der letzte Cinematic Food Guide
description: Konnichiwa, Freunde des guten Geschmacks – und willkommen in TOKIO! Diese Folge ist etwas Besonderes. Nicht nur, weil ...
full_description:
Konnichiwa, Freunde des guten Geschmacks – und willkommen in TOKIO! 🇯🇵

Diese Folge ist etwas Besonderes. Nicht nur, weil Tokio eine der eindrucksvollsten Food-Städte der Welt ist – sondern weil sich hier für uns ein Kreis schließt. Wir haben in den letzten Monaten so viel gegessen, gedreht, entdeckt und erlebt wie nie zuvor. Und jetzt stehen wir hier – zwischen Neonlichtern, Bahnlinien und winzigen Izakayas – und wissen: Diese Episode ist anders. Vielleicht ein Abschluss. Vielleicht ein Abschied.

Tokio ist eine Stadt der Extreme. Tradition trifft auf Technologie. Stille Hinterhöfe auf volle Metrostationen. Zwischen Sushi-Meistern, Spießbuden und dampfenden Straßenküchen zeigt Tokio, wie facettenreich Essen sein kann – nicht nur geschmacklich, sondern auch kulturell. Hier zählt jedes Detail, jede Bewegung, jede Zutat. Und gleichzeitig ist da diese andere Seite: laut, wild, überraschend zugänglich – mit Gurken am Spieß, Bier in Plastikbechern und einer Herzlichkeit, die man nicht erwartet. Ob Izakaya in Shinjuku, Streetfood in Asakusa, Marktstände am Ameyoko oder Omakase-Erlebnisse in versteckten Seitengassen – Tokio ist kulinarisch ein eigenes Universum. Zwischen Convenience Store Snacks und Michelin-Küche liegt oft nur eine Straßenecke. Für Foodies ist diese Stadt ein Paradies – und ein bisschen auch ein Labyrinth.

Und an diesem Punkt nochmal: Danke für eure Unterstützung, euer Feedback, eure Treue! Wie es weitergeht, können wir nicht sagen. Aber was war, war besonders. Und dieses Video vielleicht der letzte große Bissen.

Aber jetzt wollen wir wissen: Wart ihr schon mal in Tokio? Was war euer Lieblingsgericht oder überraschendster Moment? Schreibt’s in die Kommentare! 👇💬

👉 Außerdem: In den nächsten Wochen kommen noch ein paar Nachzügler aus Asien – bleibt also dran!

description: Get 10% off with my link on Squarespace - https://squarespace.com/paolofromtokyo I'm taking you to Kyoto Tower Sando hidden ...
full_description:
Get 10% off with my link on Squarespace -  https://squarespace.com/paolofromtokyo

I’m taking you to Kyoto Tower Sando hidden just below the base of the iconic Nidec Kyoto Tower and unknown to many, directly connected to Kyoto station through an underground passage.  And at the time of filming this, it’s so surprising how little to no lines there are given that the food is so reasonably priced and rivals the taste of any shops you’d find at many of Kyoto’s traveler destinations, which is why this is has become one of my go-to spots after and before getting off the train with it’s traditional Japanese dishes and unique Kyoto specialities.  Here’s the skinny, the first floor hosts souvenir shops, the second floor has a workshop type area and the basement B1 floor is the Food Hall….and where this Kyoto Food Adventure begins as I show you some of my top hitters

title: THE Hakone Guide: Everything to Do in One Day! ♨️
description: It's not just hot springs--here's the complete guide! More info: piqtour.com/hakone And check out our tours and photo shoots!
full_description:
It's not just hot springs--here's the complete guide!

More info: piqtour.com/hakone
And check out our tours and photo shoots!

Planning a trip to Hakone? This video is your complete guide to making the most of your day in this beautiful mountain town—just a short ride from Tokyo. I’ll walk you through the perfect itinerary, including must-see spots like the Hakone Open-Air Museum, Owakudani volcanic valley, the Hakone Ropeway, Lake Ashi, and the famous pirate ship cruise.

You'll also learn how to use the Hakone Freepass, tips for riding the Romancecar, and the best spots to eat and relax in hot springs. Whether you're visiting in spring, summer, autumn, or winter, this guide will help you travel smarter.
